content.full_text:
Fnrn Polrrtcel PRACTICEs CouuISSIoN
428 J Street o Suite 620 . Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-0886
May 25,2006
Dale R. Molesworth
Council Member
City of Buellton
330 W. Hwy. 246,#795
Buellton, CA 93427
RE: Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. .4.-06-080
Dear Councilman Molesworth:
This letter is in response to your request regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").r This letter is limited solely to
provisions of the Act, and should not be taken as advice regarding any other areas of the
law potentially raised by your letter.2
QUESTION
As a city council member, may you participate in city council decisions relating to
a project located within 500 feet of a mobile home park in which you reside, where the
location of your mobile home is more than 500 feet from the project?
CONCLUSION
Because you rent space from the owners of the mobile home park on a month-to-
month basis, you do not have an interest in real property that would preclude you from
participating in city council decisions regarding projects that are within 500 feet of the
mobile home park. However, if it is reasonably foreseeable that a city council decision
would affect your personal finances, including the value of your mobile home, by $250 or
more in a 12-month period, the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act would preclude
you from participating in the decision because the decision would have a material effect
upon your personal finances.
I Government Code sections 81000 - 9l}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
)-.
' This advice is applicable and confers immunity only to the extent that the facts provided to us are
conect, and that all of the material facts have been disclose d. (In re Oglesby (197 5) 1 FPPC Ops. 71 .)
,€B


File No.A-06-080
Page No. 2
F'ACTS
You are a city council member in Buellton, California. You reside in a mobile
home park of 230 units and you own your mobile home but do not own the space on
which it is located. The rent for your space is on a month-to-month basis and leases are
not available. You pay utilities, based on meter readings, directly to the park
management. You pay an annual license fee to the state but do not pay real estate taxes.
The mobile home park is privately owned and is managed and conholled by the owner.
A voluntary membership homeowner association which provide social activities exercise
no management or ownership authority. There are no CC&Rs governing your occupancy
and rent is controlledby aMobile Home Rent Control Ordinance.
The Buellton city attomey has expressed the opinion that you should not
participate in actions or discussions regarding any project that is located within 500 feet
of the mobile home park boundary,regardless of its proximity to your mobile home.
While the park boundaries are within 500 feet of the project that is the subject of a city
council decision, your mobile home is not within 500 feet of the project.
ANALYSIS
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).)
Section 87100 prohibits anypublic official from making,participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a govemmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether
a public official has a disqualifoing conflict of interest in a governmental decision.
(Regulation 18700(b).) The general rule, however, is that a conflict of interest exists
whenever a public official makes a governmental decision which has a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of his or her financial interests.
step one. Are you a "public official" within the meaning of section 87100?
Section 82048 defines a public official as "every mernber, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local government agency." You have correctly identified yourself
as a public official as a member of the city council of Buellton, which is a local
government agency.


File No.A-06-080
Page No. 3
Step Two. By participating in city council decisions involving projects within
500 feet of the mobile home park where you reside, would you be making,
participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision?
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contracfual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.L ) A public oflicial
"participates in a governmental decision" when, actingwithin the authority of his or her
position and without significant substantive or intervening review, the official negotiates,
advises, or makes recommendations to the decision maker regarding the governmental
decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a goveflrmental decision if for the purpose of influencing the
decision, the official contacts or appears before any member, officer, employee, or
consultant of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.3.)
With respect to anyproject, by deliberating, voting, committing the city to a
course of action, entering into a contractual agreement on behalf of the city, discussing
with, advising or making recoflrmendations to council members or employees of any
agency appointed by the city council or within the budgetary control of the city council,
you will be engaging in activity regulated by the Act, unless an exception applies.
Step Three. What are your economic interests? What are the possible
sources of a conflict of interest?
The economic interests from which conflicts of interest may arise are described in
section 87103 and regulations 18703-18703.5. There are six kinds of economic interests:
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he
or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more. (Section
871 03(a); regulation 1 8703. 1 (a).)
If, after completing the eight-step analysis for a particular govemmental decision,
you conclude that you have a conflict of interest with respect to a particular decision, then
we would direct your attention to regulation 18702.5(b)3
a
3 Section 87105 and regulation 18702.5 provide that if a public official who holds an office
specified in section 87200 has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, he or she
must: (1) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, verbally identify each type of economic interest
involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse
himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion andlor vote on the item. For
closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and speaking as a member ofthe public regarding personal
interests, special rules found in regulation 18702.5(c) and (d) apply. As a council member, you are a public
offrcial specified in section 87200.


a
File No.A-06-080
Page No. 4
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or
she is a director, officer, parlnet, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management. (Section 87 I 03 (d); regulation I 8703. 1 (b).)
A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she
has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b);
regulation 18703.2.)
An official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, totaling $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision. (Section 87 1 03 (c); regulation I 8703.3.)
A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her
if the gifts total $360 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section
871 03(e); regulation 187 03.4.)
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family.
This is commonly referred to as the "personal financial effects" rule. (section
87 103; regulation I 8703.5.)
a
a
o
o
While the Act defines an "interest in real property''to include any leasehold
interest inreal propertylocated inthejurisdiction (section 82033), regulation 18233
expressly excludes a periodic tenancy of one month or less from this definition. We have
previously advised that regulation 18233 applies to an official's occupancy of a residence
on a month-to-month tenancy. (Traylor Advice Letter, No. 4-95-393; Yose Advice
Letter, No. I-90-475; Bower Advice Letter, No. 4-88-050.) We have also advised that
while a mobile home itself is an asset, it does not constitute arealproperty interest
because it is considered to be personal property. (Jorgensen Advice Letter, No. A-90-
017). Accordingly, your tenancy within the mobile home park does not give rise to an
economic interest in real property.a
However, a public official always has an economic interest in his or her personal
expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family.
Your mobile home would be a personal asset.
a Based on a Commission opinion, we have previously advised that notwithstanding the express
language of regulation 18233, under certain circumstances a month-to-month tenancy would be considered
an interest in real property. (Woodworth Advice Letter, No. A-91-234; In Re Overstreet(1981) 6 FPPC
Ops. 12.) In both instances, a city ordinance limited the circumstances under which a landlord could
recover possession ofrented properly such that tenants were legally entitled to occupy the property for
periods exceeding one month. Your facts do not indicate that Buellton has any such ordinance.


File No.A-06-080
Page No. 5
Step Four. Will your economic interests be directly or indirectly involved in
decisions you will make or participate in as a city council member?
Regulation 18704.5 provides that "a public official or his or her immediate family
are deemed to be directly involved in a govemmental decision which has any financial
effect on his or her personal finances or those of his or her immediate fanily."
Accordingly, if it is reasonably foreseeable that a govemmental decision regarding a
project will have any financial effect on your personal finances, you are deemed to be
directly involved in the decision.
Step Five. Applicable Materiality Standard
"A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public official's personal finances
is material if it is at least $250 in any L2-month period." (Regulation 18705.5(a).)
Because you have an economic interest in your mobile home, a personal property asset,
you will be required to disqualiff yourself from any city council decision which could
foreseeably have the effect of increasing or decreasing the value of this asset by $250 in a
1 2-month period. (B attersby Advice Letter, No. I-9 I -034).
Step Six. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of a governmental
decision on your economic interest will meet the applicable materiality standard?
@oes a conflict of interest exist?)
Under regulation 18706, an effect upon economic interests is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. You have not
advised us of any circumstances suggesting that you will be making decisions that may
affect your personal finances to a material degree. Ultimately, whether a material
financial effect is foreseeable at the time a decision is made depends on facts and
circumstances peculiar to each case. (In re Thorner, (1975) I FppC ops. l9g.) The
foreseeability of a particular financial effect is a determination that must be left, in most
instances, to the informed judgment of the public official.
Steps Seven & Eieht Does the "public generally" exception or the o,Iegally
required participation" exception apply?
Even if a material financial effect on a public official's economic interest is
reasonably foreseeable, he or she still may not be disqualified if the financial effect of the
governmental decision on the public official's economic interest is indistinguishable from
the effect on the public generally (section 87103; regulation 187000)(7); regulation
18707(a)) or the official is legally required to participate (section 87l}3;regulation
18708). You have not presented any facts indicating that either of these exceptions is
applicable in this instance.


File No.A-06-080
Page No. 6
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (et6)
322-5660.
VJ:rd
I: \Adviceltrs\A- 06-0 8 0
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General
By: Valentina Joyce
Counsel, Legal Division

As a city council member, may you participate in city council decisions relating to
a project located within 500 feet of a mobile home park in which you reside, where the
location of your mobile home is more than 500 feet from the project?

would affect your personal finances, including the value of your mobile home, by $250 or
more in a 12-month period, the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act would preclude
you from participating in the decision because the decision would have a material effect
upon your personal finances.
)-.
' This advice is applicable and confers immunity only to the extent that the facts provided to us are
conect, and that all of the material facts have been disclose d. (In re Oglesby (197 5) 1 FPPC Ops. 71 .)
,€B

a project located within 500 feet of a mobile home park in which you reside, where the
location of your mobile home is more than 500 feet from the project?

CONCLUSION: Because you rent space from the owners of the mobile home park on a month-to-
month basis, you do not have an interest in real property that would preclude you from
participating in city council decisions regarding projects that are within 500 feet of the
mobile home park. However, if it is reasonably foreseeable that a city council decision
would affect your personal finances, including the value of your mobile home, by $250 or
more in a 12-month period, the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act would preclude
you from participating in the decision because the decision would have a material effect
upon your personal finances.

conect, and that all of the material facts have been disclose d. (In re Oglesby (197 5) 1 FPPC Ops. 71 .)
